questions.scenario:
Three DevPolaris services may need private application buckets, so the platform team proposes this first shared module call:

```hcl
module "orders_bucket" {
  source = "../../modules/s3-bucket"

  bucket = "dp-orders-invoices-prod"
}
```

Today the child contains one `aws_s3_bucket` and exposes nearly every provider argument without enforcing privacy, tags, or retention. The team wants reuse only when it reduces repeated policy decisions. What is the best review comment?

options.text: Keep the wrapper because a consistent module source can centralize future policy even if it enforces none today.
The module is probably too thin because it adds indirection without encoding a policy, pattern, or safer default.
Keep the wrapper because repeated resource syntax alone is enough value, even when callers still set every provider argument.
Reject only the local source path, then approve the same pass-through module from a registry.

questions.explanation: A module should encode a useful pattern, policy, or interface rather than only hide one provider block. Future centralization is speculative when the current contract enforces nothing, and repeated syntax alone may not justify lost plan readability. Moving the same pass-through wrapper to a registry changes distribution, not its design value. See [Terraform module configuration](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/modules/configuration).

questions.scenario: The platform team revises the proposal into `private-application-bucket`. The child creates a bucket, enforces public-access blocking and standard ownership tags, permits callers to choose versioning, and returns the name and ARN required by deployments. Why does this design better satisfy the team's reuse goal?

options.text: It is better because callers no longer need to inspect provider-level changes in the plan.
It is better because every safety setting is fixed, even if a caller has a valid exception.
It packages a repeated infrastructure pattern while keeping caller decisions like name, environment, owner, and versioning explicit.
It is better because the child module can also choose the caller's backend and production credentials.

questions.explanation: The stronger module has a real purpose: private application bucket behavior. It packages repeated safe defaults while preserving meaningful caller decisions. Callers must still review plans, valid exceptions need an intentional interface, and backend plus credential context belong to the root module rather than the reusable child.

questions.scenario: A proposed module has fifteen boolean inputs such as `block_public_acls`, `ignore_public_acls`, `restrict_public_buckets`, `enable_lifecycle`, and `force_destroy`. Which concerns are valid?

options.text: Boolean variables are invalid in Terraform modules.
A long boolean list always proves the provider resource should be deleted.
The module may lack a strong opinion if every safety behavior is pushed back to the caller.
The caller may be forced to understand provider internals that the module should have packaged as a policy.

questions.explanation: A long list of booleans can mean the module is leaking implementation details and has no clear default behavior. The caller should choose meaningful differences, not rebuild the module author's resource decisions. Boolean variables are valid, and input design does not imply resources should be deleted.

questions.scenario: The orders team wants one `all-infra` module call to create buckets, queues, IAM roles, alarms, and deployment resources in every environment. A routine alarm change would therefore evaluate the same broad module contract used for security and data resources. What is the main review and recovery risk?

options.text: Terraform cannot call modules that create more than one resource type.
Large modules always run faster because Terraform has fewer files to read.
The module is safe if it has no outputs.
The module may hide too many unrelated decisions behind one call, making plans and failures harder to understand.

questions.explanation: A broad `all-infra` module can hide many operational decisions and make review harder. Terraform can call modules with multiple resource types, but the boundary should still be readable and purposeful. File count is not the important performance signal, and removing outputs does not make a module safer.

questions.scenario: Before publishing a shared `s3-bucket` module, the platform lead asks whether its name can describe a company pattern without repeating the provider resource type. The module currently exposes most S3 arguments and enforces no standard policy. What design weakness is this naming test trying to reveal?

options.text: It tests whether the wrapper is generic enough to expose every provider argument as a caller-controlled variable.
It determines whether Terraform must run `terraform init` before resolving the child module source.
It checks whether the module source path should move from a local directory to a remote registry before reuse.
It tests whether the module describes an architecture concept, such as a private application bucket, instead of only wrapping a provider block.

questions.explanation: A useful module name often says what pattern or policy it enforces. If the best name is only `s3-bucket`, the module may be a thin wrapper. The naming test is not about local versus remote sources, exposing every argument, or initialization behavior.

questions.scenario: Production renames the module call from `module.artifact_bucket` to `module.orders_artifact_bucket` without changing the child module or bucket. The plan proposes recreating every child resource under the new module path. What is missing?

options.text: A `moved` block that records the old and new module addresses for the refactor.
A duplicate module call kept permanently so both paths manage the same resources.
A provider alias inside the child module with the old module name.
A new backend because each module call must have its own state file.

questions.explanation: The module call name is part of every child resource address in state. A `moved` block preserves those bindings during the rename. Duplicating the call would create competing ownership, and backend or provider changes do not translate module addresses. See [refactoring modules](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/modules/develop/refactoring).

questions.scenario: A proposed `private-bucket` child module declares the S3 backend, assumes the production role, and hardcodes `environment = "prod"`. Development must call the same module next month. Which boundary should reviewers restore?

options.text: Keep all production decisions in the child because reusable modules should control every caller.
Copy the child module into separate dev and prod folders so neither root passes inputs.
Remove outputs so callers cannot observe which bucket the child created.
Keep backend, provider target, and environment choices in each root module, while the child module owns the reusable bucket pattern.

questions.explanation: The root module owns the run context, backend, provider identity, environment inputs, and approval boundary. The child module contributes reusable resources to that same graph and state. Hardcoding production prevents safe reuse, while copies drift and missing outputs weaken the contract. See [Terraform module composition](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/modules/develop/composition).
